mnemonics: SR Latch: Set-Reset Latch (S for Set, R for Reset)
JK Flip-Flop: Jack-King (J for Set, K for Reset, both inputs toggles)
D Flip-Flop: Data or Delay (Output follows the D input on the clock edge)
T Flip-Flop: Toggle (T input toggles the output on the clock edge)
keyPoints: Flip-flops are the fundamental building blocks of sequential logic circuits, storing one bit of state.
They are edge-triggered (e.g., on the rising or falling clock edge), unlike latches which are level-sensitive.
The type of flip-flop (SR, D, JK, T) determines its characteristic table and how the inputs affect the next state (Q+).
commonMistakes: Confusing latches (level-sensitive) with flip-flops (edge-triggered). This is a critical timing distinction.
Forgetting that the S and R inputs on a basic SR latch are active-low, meaning they perform their function when set to 0.
Incorrectly applying the 'race around' condition in a JK flip-flop when both J and K are 1 for the entire clock pulse, which is solved with edge-triggering or master-slave design.
